question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the distinct roots of $a x^2+b x+c=0$, then $\lim _{x \rightarrow \alpha} \frac{1-\cos \left(a x^2+b x+c\right)}{(x-\alpha)^2}$ is equal to

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Given limit }=\lim _{x \rightarrow \alpha} \frac{1-\cos a(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)}{(x-\alpha)^2}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \alpha} \frac{2 \sin ^2\left(a \frac{(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)}{2}\right)}{(x-\alpha)^2} \\
& =\lim _{x \rightarrow \alpha} \frac{2}{(x-\alpha)^2} \times \frac{\sin ^2\left(a \frac{(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)}{2}\right)}{a^2(x-\alpha)^2(x-\beta)^2} \times \frac{a^2(x-\alpha)^2(x-\beta)^2}{4} \\
& =\frac{a^2(\alpha-\beta)^2}{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

q: If $y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\cos x - 4\sin x}{4\cos x + 3\sin x}\right) + 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{1+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at $x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is equal to:
question: If $y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\cos x - 4\sin x}{4\cos x + 3\sin x}\right) + 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{1+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at $x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $y = y_1 + y_2$

$y_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\cos x - 4\sin x}{4\cos x + 3\sin x}\right)$

Dividing the numerator and denominator by $4\cos x$:

$y_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{3}{4} - \tan x}{1 + \dfrac{3}{4}\tan x}\right)$

Using the formula $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{A - B}{1 + AB}\right) = \tan^{-1}A - \tan^{-1}B$:

$y_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}(\tan x) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) - x$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$\dfrac{dy_1}{dx} = -1$

Now, $y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{1+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$

Substituting $x = \sin \theta$:

$y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sin \theta}{1+\cos \theta}\right)$

$y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)}{2\cos^2(\theta/2)}\right)$

$y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\tan\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = 2\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \theta = \sin^{-1}x$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$\dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$

At $x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$\dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{3}{4}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}}} = 2$

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy_1}{dx} + \dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = -1 + 2 = 1$

Answer: $1$

question: The parabola $y^2=x$ divides the circle $x^2+y^2=2$ into two parts whose areas are in the ratio
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Area of circle }=\pi(\sqrt{2})^2=2 \pi \\ & \text { Area of } O C A D O=2\{\text { Area of } O C A O\} \\ & \quad=2 \text { area of } O C B+\text { area of } B C A\} \\ & =2 \int_0^1 y_p d x+2 \int_1^{\sqrt{2}} y_c d x \\ & \text { where } y_p=\sqrt{x} \text { and } y_c=\sqrt{2-x^2} \\ & \therefore \text { Required Area } \\ & =2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x+2 \int_0^{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{2-x^2} d x \\ & =2\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot 1-0\right]+2\left[\frac{x \sqrt{2-x^2}}{2}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right]_1^{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{4}{3}+2\left\{\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right\}=\frac{4}{3}+2\left\{\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right\}=\frac{3 \pi+2}{6} \\ & \text { Bigger area }=2 \pi-\frac{3 \pi+2}{6}=\frac{9 \pi-2}{6} \\ & \therefore \text { Required Ratio }=\frac{9 \pi-2}{3 \pi+2} \text { i.e., } 9 \pi-2: 3 \pi+2 \end{aligned} $$

2b2-16b2+14=0

So, 5k-1-3k+2+k=0

⇒k=1

∴ α+β+γ=9k+1=10

q: If a line intercepted between the coordinate axes is trisected at a point $\mathrm{A}(4,3)$, which is nearer to $x$-axis, then its equation is:
question: If a line intercepted between the coordinate axes is trisected at a point $\mathrm{A}(4,3)$, which is nearer to $x$-axis, then its equation is:
solution: $$ \text { A divides } \mathrm{CB} \text { in } 2: 1 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow 4=\left(\frac{1 \times 0+2 \times a}{1+2}\right)=\frac{2 a}{3} \\ &\Rightarrow a=6 \Rightarrow \text { coordinate of B is B }(6,0) \\ &3=\left(\frac{1 \times b+2 \times 0}{1+2}\right)=\frac{b}{3} \\ &\Rightarrow b=9 \text { and } \mathrm{C}(0,9) \end{aligned} $$ Slope of line passing through $(6,0),(0,9)$ slope, $m=\frac{9}{-6}=-\frac{3}{2}$ Equation of line $y-0=\frac{-3}{2}(x-6)$ $$ \begin{aligned} &2 y=-3 x+18 \\ &3 x+2 y=18 \end{aligned} $$

q: The distance of the point having position vector <math><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> from the straight line passing through the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and parallel to the vector, <math><mn>6</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is
question: The distance of the point having position vector <math><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> from the straight line passing through the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and parallel to the vector, <math><mn>6</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is
solution:
Let, the given point be P≡-i^+2j^+6k^ and A≡2, 3, -4, hence the position vector of the point A is 2i^+3j^-4k^.

We know that the vector joining two points x1, y1, z1 and x2, y2, z2 is x2-x1i^+y2-y1j^+z2-z1k^,

Hence, the vector AP→=-1-2i^+2-3j^+6+4k^

⇒AP→=-3i^-j^+10k^

And, AD is the projection of AP→ on the given line i.e. 6i^+3j^-4k^

We know that the projection of a vector a→ on a vector b→ is a→·b→b→

Hence, AD=AP→⋅n→n→

⇒AD=-3i^-j^+10k^·6i^+3j^-4k^62+32+-42

⇒AD=-18-3-4036+9+16

⇒AD=61 units

The distance between the points x1, y1, z1 and x2, y2, z2 is x1-x22+y1-y22+z1-z22

Thus, AP=-1-22+2-32+6+42=110 units

Now, in triangle ADP, by Pythagoras theorem, we have AP2=PD2+AD2

⇒PD=AP2-AD2=110-61=7 units.

q: The foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the line joining the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> lies on the plane
question: The foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the line joining the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> lies on the plane
solution:
Equation of a line through given points 1, -2, 3 and 1, 1, 0 will be

x-10=y+23=z-3-3=λ.

Direction ratio of line is =3j^-3k^.

A point on this line can be taken as 1, 3λ-2, -3λ+3.

Let this point is foot of perpendicular from point 4, 2, 3.

Direction ratio of line joining these points is =3i^+4-3λj^+3λk^.

Both lines are perpendicular to each other.

⇒Dot product of directions ratio will be zero.

⇒3j^-3k^·3i^+4-3λj^+3λk^=0

⇒0+34-3λ+-33λ=0

⇒18λ=12 ⇒λ=23

Replace value of λ for foot of perpendicular.

⇒Foot of perpendicular is 1, 0, 1.

This point satisfies the plane 2x+y-z=1.

q: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \operatorname{cosec} x\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}-\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)$ is:
question: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \operatorname{cosec} x\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}-\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)$ is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \operatorname{cosec}\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}-\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right) \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\cos \operatorname{ec} x\left(\cos ^2 x+3 \cos x-\sin x-4\right)}{\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\sin x} \frac{\left(\cos ^2 x+3 \cos x-4\right)-\sin x}{\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(\cos ^x+4\right)(\cos x-1)-\sin x}{\sin x\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}(\cos x+4)-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\left(\sin ^x(\cos x+4)+\cos \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\cos \frac{x}{2}\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & -\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{5}}\end{aligned}$
